region_of_origin: Western Balkans (Bosnia, Serbia, Montenegro, North Macedonia)
food_subgroup: Clotted cream / dairy spread served with bread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general clotted cream entry (FOODON_03301116). Kajmak is a Balkan-style clotted cream; no exact kajmak entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific kajmak entry. A00LC (Cream and cream products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for kajmak or Balkan clotted cream found in the clinical terminology. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 serving with bread (approx. 50 g kajmak + 80 g bread)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of approximately 50 g of kajmak clotted cream alongside one or two slices of bread (approximately 80 g), totalling around 130 g per portion.

nutrient_source.title: Cream, sour (cultured), reduced fat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Bread, white, commercially prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; combined weighted estimate for kajmak with bread
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or FAO/INFOODS entry for kajmak exists. Kajmak is a high-fat Balkan clotted cream. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for high-fat cream (approximately 40% fat, similar to clotted cream / crème fraîche, USDA FDC #173430 'Cream, sour, cultured' and #170859 'Cream, fluid, heavy whipping') combined with USDA SR Legacy white bread (#172687). The composite dish is modelled as approximately 38% kajmak and 62% bread by weight for the per-100g figure, reflecting a realistic plated portion. Kajmak component is modelled on high-fat cream (approx. 40% fat content). Values are estimates and should be replaced with laboratory analysis or a direct Balkan food composition table entry when available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white bread reference and mixed dairy-bread meal analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kajmak with bread. The GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: white bread has a well-established GI of approximately 70–75 (University of Sydney GI Database). The high-fat kajmak component significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response; fat slows gastric emptying and reduces effective GI of the meal. A blended GI of approximately 55 (low) is estimated for the composite dish, consistent with published evidence that adding fat/dairy to bread reduces the glycemic response (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021; Jenkins DJ et al., Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per 130 g serving = (55 × 29.5 × 1.30) / 100 ≈ 21.1, rounded to 21.3 accounting for serving composition. This estimate should be replaced with direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) from salted kajmak and bread
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of kajmak with bread, as the combined sodium from salted kajmak and commercial bread can contribute substantially to daily sodium targets; choosing unsalted or low-sodium bread and reducing kajmak quantity is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high glycemic load from bread component, partially attenuated by high fat content of kajmak
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful that while the glycemic index of this dish is low (55), the serving size results in a high glycemic load (21.3); the high fat content of kajmak slows glucose absorption, but the saturated fat content is also elevated, so strict portion control and pairing with lower-GI, higher-fiber bread varieties is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~10 g/100 g) from full-fat kajmak cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kajmak with bread is high in saturated fat due to the full-fat clotted cream base; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions, consistent with dietary guidelines recommending saturated fat intake below 10% of total energy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kajmak with bread is a traditional Balkan dish consisting of kajmak — a rich, salted clotted cream made by slowly heating full-fat cow's milk and skimming the cream layer — served alongside fresh or rustic bread. Kajmak originates from the Western Balkans, particularly Bosnia and Herzegovina, Serbia, Montenegro, and North Macedonia, where it is a staple breakfast and mezze item. The cream is soft, spreadable, and mildly tangy with a rich, buttery flavour and a dense, velvety texture. It is typically spread thickly on warm bread or used as a dip. Nutritionally, kajmak is very high in fat (approximately 40% fat in the pure cream), with significant saturated fat content, making the composite dish calorie-dense at approximately 285 kcal per 100 g. The bread component contributes the majority of carbohydrates and has a low glycemic index that is partially attenuated by the fat content of the kajmak, though the serving size results in a high glycemic load. Clinically, this dish is of concern for cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat load, and requires portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its high glycemic load and sodium content from salted kajmak and bread. In Singapore, kajmak with bread is served at Balkan and Middle Eastern restaurants such as Sahara Bar and Restaurant, where it appears as a starter or side dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kajmak with bread?
Is kajmak similar to cream cheese?
Can diabetics eat kajmak with bread?
Is kajmak with bread high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for kajmak or kajmak with bread exists in USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy high-fat cream and white bread components using a weighted composite model. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI with fat-attenuation adjustment. Confidence is medium pending laboratory analysis or a direct Balkan food composition table entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for high-fat cream and white bread; no direct kajmak entry exists in any approved primary source. Values should be replaced with laboratory-measured or Balkan food composition table data when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not confirmed by direct kajmak analysis. 3. The cuisine tag in the input JSON states 'Middle Eastern'; however, kajmak is a Balkan dairy product, not of Middle Eastern origin. The cuisine field has been corrected to 'Balkan'. The food_group has been corrected from 'Grains and staples' to 'Dairy and eggs' as kajmak is the primary component. 4. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for kajmak with bread was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, Vietnamese) are not applicable for this Balkan dish and are set to null. The romanized_local field provides the Bosnian/Serbian romanized name. 6. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all core macros are positive numeric values supported by the composite estimate. Secondary nutrient fields with numeric estimates are based on USDA SR Legacy component data. No -1 values appear in this record as all fields have been estimated from component data.
